topic: Remote work killed company culture. Discuss.

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Welcome to The Arena, I am so pumped you're here for this one. Ben Horowitz — co-founder of a16z, author of The Hard Thing About Hard Things, and someone who has literally written the book on what culture actually is and why it lives or dies. Brian Halligan — co-founder of HubSpot, a company that became a remote-first pioneer and somehow landed on Best Places to Work lists while doing it. The question hanging over every leadership team right now: did we trade something irreplaceable for flexibility, or did the office just become a crutch we were too comfortable to question? Ben, I'll start with you — is culture even possible without physical proximity?

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Look, the framing of this question is wrong, and that's actually the most important thing to address first.

Remote work didn't kill company culture. **Lazy thinking about culture killed company culture** — and remote work just made the laziness impossible to hide.

Here's what I mean. When I was building Loudcloud and Opsware, culture wasn't something that happened because people were sitting next to each other eating lunch. Culture is the set of behaviors that get rewarded and punished in your organization. It's what your people do when you're not in the room — or in this case, not in the building. Most companies were never as intentional about culture as they thought they were. They confused proximity with alignment. They confused shared office space with shared values. When the office disappeared in 2020, they discovered they'd been coasting on physical co-location as a substitute for actual cultural architecture. That's not remote work's fault. That's a management failure.

Now, here's where I'll give the other side something real: there are specific functions of culture that physical presence makes dramatically easier. The transmission of tacit knowledge — how we actually make decisions, how we handle a crisis, what "good" looks like in this organization — that stuff travels fastest through observation and informal interaction. When I think about what I wrote in *The Hard Thing About Hard Things*, the whole point was that the hard parts of building a company are *relational* and *judgment-based*, not process-based. Those are exactly the capabilities that attenuate over distance. So the question I'd push back to anyone defending fully remote isn't "can you maintain culture?" — it's "**can you transmit culture to people who never experienced it being built?**" That's the harder problem nobody has solved yet.

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Lenny Rachitsky: So speaking of advice that you've shared that might be out of date now, you are famous for writing one of the most popular

turns.speaker: Brian Halligan
turns.text:
Let me push back on the framing here, because "killed" is too binary — but I'll tell you this: remote work absolutely created a culture crisis that most companies are still sleepwalking through.

Here's the thing. Culture isn't a memo. Culture isn't a Notion doc with your values written down. Culture is an organism — it's alive, it needs to be tended to constantly, it needs oxygen. And as I talked about on Lenny's podcast, if you want to kill a plant, have two people water it. What remote work did was essentially give everyone on your leadership team shared responsibility for culture with no clear ownership, no clear mechanism, and no physical proximity to create the feedback loops you need. The plant didn't get watered. Or it got drowned in Zoom happy hours. Either way, the plant's dead.

When I look at HubSpot — and I spent a long time building that culture from the ground up — the companies that have maintained strong cultures through this era are the ones that treated culture with the same rigor they treat product. They made deliberate choices. They didn't just assume culture would survive because they still had good people. At HubSpot, half the management team had been there for what felt like 150 years, and that continuity was a feature, not an accident. Remote work made that kind of cultural continuity exponentially harder to build and nearly impossible to transmit to new hires who've never experienced what "good" looks like in person.

So here's my challenge to the other side: don't tell me about the companies where remote work is thriving. Tell me how a 22-year-old joining your company remotely is supposed to absorb your culture, find their mentors, and understand what excellence looks like — when the best teachers are heads-down on Slack and nobody's eating lunch together. That's the problem that hasn't been solved yet.

sources.title: Sequoia CEO coach: Why it’s never been easier to start a company, and never been harder to scale one | Brian Halligan (co-founder, HubSpot)
sources.snippet: I love this one, it's very true. Let's say, Lenny, you bought a new beautiful plant for your office, and then you went away for a month to T

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Brian, I want to press you on something. You said culture is "an organism that needs oxygen" — and I love that framing — but HubSpot has been held up as a remote-first success story. So which is it? Either remote work suffocates that organism, or your own company is evidence that it doesn't. You can't have it both ways.

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Brian, you're making a really important point about the 22-year-old, and I want to take it seriously because it's the strongest version of the argument. But I think you're conflating two different problems. The challenge of transmitting culture to new hires isn't a remote work problem — it's a *codification* problem. When I wrote The Hard Thing About Hard Things, one of the core things I was trying to get at is that most founders never actually define what their culture is. They just assume people will absorb it by osmosis. That works fine when you're 30 people in one room. It was already broken at 300 people in one office. Remote work didn't create that failure mode — it just exposed it faster and more brutally.

Here's where I'll push back directly on your "plant" analogy. You said remote work gave everyone shared responsibility for culture with no clear ownership. But that's a leadership design failure, not a geography failure. At Opsware, I had to make culture explicit and deliberate because we were fighting for survival — there was no luxury of assuming the hallway conversations were doing the cultural work. The companies that are failing at remote culture are the same companies that would have had mediocre cultures in person. They were coasting on proximity. What I've seen at the best a16z portfolio companies is that the ones thriving remotely had founders who could actually articulate — specifically, not in values-speak — what behaviors they were rewarding and what behaviors they were penalizing. That's the whole game.

So here's my challenge back to you: HubSpot's success is actually evidence *for* my argument, not yours. You built a culture document — the Culture Code — that became one of the most downloaded documents in the history of Silicon Valley. You *codified* it. You made it explicit. That's why it survived the remote transition. So the question I'd pose is: if a company's culture can't survive being written down and transmitted deliberately, was it ever a real culture — or was it just a vibe held together by free lunch?

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Yeah. It's kind of the more advanced book. You kind of have to survive to want to care about dealing with the cultural issues. And so the su

turns.speaker: Brian Halligan
turns.text:
Ben, you're half right, and I'll give you that — which is exactly why this is interesting. The Culture Code wasn't an accident. Dharmesh and I built that document because we knew culture at scale requires codification. You cannot whisper your values into existence. So yes, writing it down matters enormously. But here's where your argument has a blind spot: the Culture Code was *born* in an office environment. It codified what was already alive and breathing in the room. The harder question isn't whether you can transmit culture through a document — you can — it's whether you can *generate* culture through a document. Those are two completely different problems, and conflating them is where I think you're getting loose.

Here's what I saw inside HubSpot during the pandemic transition, and I'll be direct about it: we didn't just flip a switch and the culture survived because we had a good PDF. It was two steps forward, one step back — which is honestly how everything hard works inside a company. We had to reinvent how culture gets built, not just transmitted. The 22-year-old issue is real not because they can't read the values document — they can read it in 20 minutes — but because culture is also behavioral, it's tacit, it's watching how a senior person handles a crisis or a missed quarter in real time, in the room, and then internalizing it. Zoom doesn't replicate that fidelity. And I'd argue the companies that claim they've fully solved this remotely are either exceptional outliers or they haven't fully reckoned with what they've lost yet.

So here's my challenge back to you: your codification argument is necessary but not sufficient. The best culture documents in the world — and I'd put the HubSpot Culture Code up against anything — are artifacts of a living culture, not substitutes for one. If your argument is that great remote companies have just been more disciplined about codification, I'd say look at the a16z portfolio five years from now and tell me which ones built *generationally sticky* cultures versus which ones built cultures that look great on an internal wiki. The jury is genuinely still out on whether remote-first companies can create that next generation of culture carriers. Can you point to one that's done it at scale, through a full generational turnover of employees?

sources.title: Sequoia CEO coach: Why it’s never been easier to start a company, and never been harder to scale one | Brian Halligan (co-founder, HubSpot)
sources.snippet: Yeah, I always thought, incorrectly, that we would have one hire or one investor or one event or one product release that would... I was wro

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text:
Here's my hot take that neither of you has touched: the companies screaming loudest about returning to the office aren't doing it for culture — they're doing it for control. And those are two completely different things, and we've been conflating them for three years.

I'll be honest, I used to think in-person was obviously better for culture until I started looking at the data on who's actually thriving remotely. Turns out the companies winning at remote culture are the ones who were intentional before remote was forced on them. The ones struggling? They were coasting on proximity as a substitute for actual management.

So here's the question I want both of you to answer: if your company has to physically co-locate people to maintain its culture, is that a culture — or is it just surveillance with snacks?

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Brian, you're making a genuinely sharp distinction — generating culture versus transmitting it — and I want to sit with that for a second because it's the right question. You're correct that the HubSpot Culture Code codified something that was already alive. I'll give you that. But here's where I think your argument quietly assumes its own conclusion: you're treating the office as the *only* generative environment for culture, when really what you had in those early HubSpot rooms was **intensity, shared stakes, and behavioral modeling under pressure**. Those things can exist remotely. They're just harder to manufacture, and most companies are too lazy to do the work.

Here's the framework I keep coming back to, which I wrote about in *What You Do Is Who You Are*: culture isn't what you say, it's what you *do* when it's hard. Toussaint L'Ouverture didn't build the Haitian revolutionary culture through proximity — he built it through a series of incredibly difficult decisions that became legend, that became *story*, and story traveled. The behavioral modeling your 22-year-old needs isn't about being in the room — it's about whether the senior person's behavior in the crisis is *visible and interpretable*. Great remote cultures have figured out how to make those moments legible. Most remote cultures haven't. That's a leadership failure, not a structural impossibility.

So here's my challenge back to you: you asked me to name a remote-first company that's built generationally sticky culture through full employee turnover. Fair ask. But I'd flip it — can you name an *office-first* company that didn't have to completely reinvent its culture after a major leadership transition or scaling event? Because if the office were really the generative engine you're describing, that shouldn't happen as often as it does. Culture fragility at scale isn't a remote work problem. It's a *who carries the culture when the founders leave* problem — and that's true whether your people are in a WeWork or on Zoom.

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Yeah. It's kind of the more advanced book. You kind of have to survive to want to care about dealing with the cultural issues. And so the su

turns.speaker: Brian Halligan
turns.text:
Ben, you're making a sharp point and I'll concede part of it — you're right that I've been treating the office as the *only* generative environment, and that's too strong. The real ingredients are intensity, shared stakes, and behavioral modeling under pressure. I'll own that framing. But here's where your Toussaint example actually cuts against your argument more than you realize: his culture traveled through *story* precisely because those moments were so dramatically visible that they couldn't be misread. The default setting of remote work is ambiguity — a senior leader making a hard call on Slack at 11pm is nearly invisible. A senior leader making that same call in a room where twelve people watch them choose the harder right over the easier wrong? That becomes legend inside a company. That becomes the story that gets retold at onboarding three years later. The question isn't whether remote *can* make those moments legible — it's whether it does, and the honest answer is: almost never, because it requires deliberate effort that most leadership teams aren't willing to spend.

On your flip challenge — can I name an office-first company that didn't have to reinvent culture after major leadership transition? No, and I wouldn't try. That's a completely fair point. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, one of the things I'm proudest of at HubSpot is that half the management team were people who'd been there for what felt like 150 years — homegrown leaders who *were* the culture carriers. That's the real answer to your "who carries it when founders leave" question. It's not the office, it's the depth of tenure on the leadership team. But here's the thing — remote work is actively destroying that flywheel. Attrition is higher in fully remote environments, the connective tissue is weaker, and the people who self-select to leave first are often exactly the mid-level culture carriers you need most.

So let me sharpen the actual disagreement between us: you're arguing this is a leadership failure, not a structural problem. I'm arguing the structure makes the leadership failure dramatically more likely. Remote work doesn't kill culture — it raises the degree of difficulty by an order of magnitude, and most leadership teams are already operating at the edge of their capacity. That's not a coincidence. That's a design flaw. So here's my challenge back to you: if this is purely a codification and leadership problem, why are the companies with the strongest reputations for culture post-pandemic — the ones people actually want to work at — almost uniformly the ones who figured out a hybrid model rather than going fully remote?

sources.title: Sequoia CEO coach: Why it’s never been easier to start a company, and never been harder to scale one | Brian Halligan (co-founder, HubSpot)
sources.snippet: Okay, EV is enterprise value, TV is your team's value, MEV is your value. And as HubSpot was scaling, and we had a lot of people who were VP

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text:
Okay, let me try to land this plane for a second.

Here's where I think you two actually agree: culture requires codification, it requires behavioral modeling under pressure, and it requires shared stakes. Nobody's arguing against any of that. That's real common ground.

Here's where you fundamentally differ: Brian thinks the office is where those ingredients most naturally and reliably come together, especially at the generative stage. Ben thinks the ingredients are portable — that if you're intentional enough, you can reconstruct the conditions anywhere. That's the real disagreement, and it's a meaningful one.

And here's the question neither of you has fully answered: what do you do about the company that didn't start remote-first, that has a culture that was built in-person, and is now trying to maintain it across a hybrid mess? Because that's not a thought experiment — that's like ninety percent of companies right now.

Here's where

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Brian, you're sharpening the disagreement in exactly the right place, and I want to engage with it directly. Your claim is that remote raises the degree of difficulty, not that it's impossible. Fine. I'll largely accept that framing. But then your closing question — why do the strongest culture companies post-pandemic tend to be hybrid? — actually proves my point more than yours. Hybrid isn't the office. Hybrid is a deliberate leadership choice to create specific conditions for culture transmission. Which means those companies succeeded not because they had more square footage, but because their leadership teams made explicit, effortful decisions about when and how culture needed to be built. That's the codification and intentionality argument I've been making the whole time, dressed in a different set of clothes.

On the visibility point — the idea that a hard call made on Slack at 11pm is invisible and a hard call made in a conference room becomes legend — I think you're describing a real phenomenon but misidentifying the cause. The reason the conference room moment becomes a story is not the room. It's because someone *narrated it*. Someone made it meaningful and passed it along. When I was building Loudcloud and we were making brutal decisions about what to cut and what to keep alive, the moments that became part of our culture weren't the ones that happened in physical proximity — they were the ones that got told and retold. The oral tradition of a company is a leadership responsibility, not an architectural feature. If your senior leaders are making hard calls on Slack and nobody's narrating that, the failure is on the leadership team for not understanding that culture lives in stories.

Here's where I'll actually push back hardest: your attrition argument has a selection effect baked into it. Companies that went fully remote chaotically, with no intentionality, with founders who didn't understand what they were losing — those companies have higher attrition and weaker culture. But that's not evidence that remote work causes culture decay. That's evidence that bad leadership causes culture decay, and remote work exposes bad leadership faster. So here's my challenge back to you: can you show me a company with genuinely strong leadership — leaders who understood what they were building and made deliberate choices — that went fully remote and *still* watched the culture collapse? Because if the variable is leadership quality, not physical location, then we're not having a debate about remote work at all. We're having a debate about whether most leadership teams are actually good enough to run a company in the first place.

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Lenny Rachitsky: So speaking of advice that you've shared that might be out of date now, you are famous for writing one of the most popular

turns.speaker: Brian Halligan
turns.text:
Ben, your selection effect argument is sharp, and I want to engage with it honestly — because you're right that bad leadership was going to produce culture decay regardless of zip code. I'll grant you that. But here's the problem with your challenge: you're asking me to produce a counterexample where *strong* leadership *and* full remote *still* produced collapse. That's almost unfalsifiable by design, because strong leadership teams, by definition, course-correct. They see the culture fraying and they pull people back together — often physically. The companies I watched at HubSpot's scale that went fully remote with serious intent and good leadership mostly... went hybrid. They didn't stay fully remote. That retreat itself is data. Leaders with good instincts kept feeling something was off and made structural changes. The migration pattern is the signal.

On the narration point — this is where I think you're technically right but practically wrong. Yes, someone has to narrate the hard call to make it cultural currency. I absolutely agree stories are the transmission mechanism. But here's what I observed building HubSpot from zero to scale: the narration happened *naturally* in physical proximity and required *deliberate engineering* in remote environments. When someone watched me or Dharmesh make a hard call in a room, five people saw it, and the story spread organically through hallways, lunches, and offsites. Remote requires you to manufacture that distribution intentionally — which adds leadership overhead that compounds across hundreds of decisions per quarter. You're not wrong that it's possible. You're underestimating how much extra organizational bandwidth it consumes.

Here's the real question I'd put back to you: we've been debating the ceiling of what great remote-first leadership can achieve, and I think you've made a credible case that the ceiling is high. But what about the median? As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, scaling a company is already the hardest version of the problem — starting is easier than scaling. If remote raises the degree of difficulty for *culture transmission* even by 20%, and most leadership teams are already operating at the edge of their capacity, doesn't that 20% matter enormously at the population level? You're arguing from the exceptional case. I'm arguing about what happens to the distribution.
